prompt:
I want a proper maintenance-plan runner, `stagerun.ps1` in the repo root, and I want the error handling done RIGHT this time — the last one died on the first bad file, left its lock behind, and we spent a morning wondering why nothing would run. Interface: `pwsh -NoProfile -File stagerun.ps1 -Plan <plan.json> -Work <dir>`. The plan is a JSON array of steps `{"name": ..., "action": ..., "arg": ...}` where arg is a path relative to the work area. Four actions: `read` (read a text file, report `chars=<n>`), `sum` (parse every non-blank line of the file as an integer, report `sum=<n>`), `scan` (count files directly in a directory, report `files=<n>`), `pull` (Get-Content the file, report `lines=<n>`).

The rules:

- A failing step must NEVER kill the run. Catch it, classify it, keep going. Classification is by exception type, with typed catch handlers, and the report shows the real exception type name (what `$_.Exception.GetType().Name` gives you): FileNotFoundException -> missing-file, ItemNotFoundException -> missing-item, DirectoryNotFoundException -> missing-dir, FormatException -> bad-number, anything else (including a step whose action nobody recognizes) -> unexpected.
- Watch your `$ErrorActionPreference`: the `pull` action goes through a cmdlet, and cmdlet errors are non-terminating by default — if you don't handle that deliberately, that failure sails right past your catch blocks. Document what you do about it in a comment.
- Handled failures are REPORT content, not console noise: stderr stays completely empty on a run that executes, even one where five steps fail.
- Stdout, exactly: first line `stage run: <plan file leaf name>`; per step in plan order `ok <name> <detail>` or `fail <name> <ExceptionTypeName>`; then, only if something failed, a `failures:` line followed by one row per failure in order, formatted with `'{0,-12} {1,-28} {2}' -f name, typeName, category`; last line `steps: <n> ok: <x> fail: <y>`.
- Locking and cleanup: take `<work>/stage.lock` before the first step. If it already exists, print `stagerun: lock present: <path>` to stderr, exit 75, and touch NOTHING (not the lock, not the status). When a run executes, the lock must be gone afterwards and `<work>/last.status` must contain `steps=<n> ok=<x> fail=<y>` plus a newline — on every exit path, failures included. That's a finally job, not a hope.
- Plan file missing: `stagerun: plan not found: <path>` to stderr, exit 66. Plan file that isn't valid JSON: `stagerun: plan is not valid JSON: <path>` to stderr, exit 65. Neither creates a lock nor writes a status.
- Exit 0 when every step succeeded, 70 when the run executed but at least one step failed.

Acceptance: `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_stagerun.ps1` from the workspace root must pass unmodified. Don't touch the test file.
notes: 6P8 item 5: robust runner. All catalog elements present: try/catch/finally with typed exception filters (five typed handlers), error-record inspection table printing $_.Exception.GetType().Name alongside the category, documented $ErrorActionPreference handling (pull uses Get-Content, whose error is non-terminating under the default preference — probed on this box 2026-07-16: try/catch misses it under Continue, catches ItemNotFoundException under Stop), cleanup-always-runs pinned via sentinel files (stage.lock released + last.status written in finally on both the all-ok and five-failures paths; the held-lock case pins that a foreign lock and prior status survive untouched). All exception type names probed, not assumed: FileNotFoundException ([IO.File]::ReadAllText), FormatException ([int]::Parse), DirectoryNotFoundException ([IO.Directory]::GetFiles), ItemNotFoundException (Get-Content -LiteralPath under Stop), NotSupportedException (thrown for unknown actions, lands in the generic handler as 'unexpected'). PowerShell-native pins with no bash analog: typed catch clauses matching .NET exception hierarchies and the terminating/non-terminating cmdlet error distinction. Differentiation: ps-nightsweep (6P5) is a DEBUG seed about error semantics gone wrong; this is the BUILD sibling — the contract demands the discipline up front. Exit-code vocabulary overlaps ps-shipgate (6P8 item 6) only in using sysexits numbers; that seed's contract is single-line stderr diagnostics with silent success, this one's is a rich stdout report with sentinel cleanup.
